title: UC2 — A new mission-control replica won't land on the launch pad
description: Today's mission for the Artemis pad shift: bring up a replacement mission-control replica on the launch pad, then hand the diagnosis to artemis-launch-pad-debugger from the operational CLI.

steps.title: Mission setup — bring up the replacement replica
steps.explanation:
Mission control needs a replacement `mission-control` replica on the Artemis launch pad — the previous one rotated out at end of shift. Apply the bundle below to your vCluster: a namespace, RBAC for the launch-pad readiness sweep, the readiness sweep itself, a service, the new replica deployment, and the on-call debugger agent that'll be addressable from the operational CLI when you need it. Once the applies complete, the replacement should be coming up on the cluster.

Six files are dropped into your workspace by this step (`fileEdits`): five manifests under `uc2/manifests/` and one Agent CRD under `uc2/agents/`. The two `kubectl apply -f` commands below send them to the cluster in order.

fileEdits.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
    kagent-workshop/scenario: pod-pending

# RBAC for the bootstrap-launch-pad-fault Job (20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml).
#
# The Job needs two cluster-scoped + namespaced permissions:
#   - patch nodes  → to apply the synthetic taint via `kubectl taint`
#   - patch deploy → to `kubectl rollout restart` mission-control after the taint,
#                    which closes the cold-deploy race that would otherwise let a
#                    Pod bind to the node before the taint lands (NFR-003).
#
# Both permissions are scoped to the absolute minimum verbs required.
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc2-bootstrap-node-tainter
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["nodes"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc2-bootstrap-node-tainter
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: artemis-uc2-bootstrap-node-tainter
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
    namespace: artemis-uc2
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
    namespace: artemis-uc2

# bootstrap-launch-pad-fault — applies the synthetic node taint that blocks
# mission-control from scheduling, then forces a rollout restart so the Pod
# is re-evaluated against the now-tainted node (closes the cold-deploy race
# where the scheduler binds the Pod before the taint lands — NFR-003).
#
# Idempotency:
#   - `kubectl taint --overwrite` is idempotent on identical key=value:effect.
#   - `kubectl rollout restart` always succeeds; if no Pod is currently scheduled
#     it just bumps the Deployment's pod template hash, producing a new Pending
#     Pod that hits the taint deterministically.
#   - The Job itself is named, not generateName'd; re-applying the same manifest
#     is a no-op ("unchanged"). To re-run, delete the Job first.
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  backoffLimit: 4
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 120
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 600
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
        app: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
      restartPolicy: OnFailure
      # Tolerate the taint we apply — required for idempotent re-runs on a
      # cluster where a previous UC2 (or UC4) cycle has already tainted the
      # node. Without this, re-applying the manifest deadlocks: the Job pod
      # itself can't schedule because of the taint, so the taint can't be
      # refreshed. Mirrored from UC4's bootstrap Job (STORY-024 finding,
      # STORY-029 retrofit).
      tolerations:
        - key: artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
        - name: tainter
          # `apogasa/kubectl:latest` is busybox-based (Alpine) — STORY-015 originally
          # referenced :1.31.0 but that tag was never published. STORY-029 retrofitted
          # to the actually-published :latest to match UC4's bootstrap Job.
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/kubectl:latest
          # /bin/sh — the busybox-based image has no /bin/bash. The script is
          # POSIX-compatible (for / if / sleep), and busybox sh supports
          # `set -o pipefail`. Mirrored from UC4's bootstrap Job (STORY-024
          # finding, STORY-029 retrofit).
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-eu", "-o", "pipefail", "-c"]
          args:
            - |
              echo "[bootstrap] tainting all nodes with artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule"
              kubectl taint nodes --all artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule --overwrite

              echo "[bootstrap] waiting up to 60s for deployment/mission-control to exist in artemis-uc2"
              for i in $(seq 1 60); do
                if kubectl get -n artemis-uc2 deploy/mission-control >/dev/null 2>&1; then
                  echo "[bootstrap] deployment found after ${i}s"
                  break
                fi
                sleep 1
              done

              if ! kubectl get -n artemis-uc2 deploy/mission-control >/dev/null 2>&1; then
                echo "[bootstrap] deployment did not appear within 60s — taint applied; exiting"
                exit 0
              fi

              echo "[bootstrap] forcing rollout restart of mission-control to surface the untolerated-taint event"
              kubectl rollout restart -n artemis-uc2 deploy/mission-control

              echo "[bootstrap] done"
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "32Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  replicas: 1
  # Recreate strategy: kill old pod first, then create new pod. Required because
  # the bootstrap Job triggers `kubectl rollout restart` AFTER the taint lands,
  # but the default RollingUpdate strategy waits for the new Pod to become Ready
  # before terminating the old one — which never happens, since the new Pod is
  # Pending forever. Recreate sidesteps the deadlock; the brief no-Pod window
  # between scale-down and scale-up is acceptable for this scenario (the
  # participant sees one Pending Pod after the Job completes, not a
  # stale-Running + Pending pair). Mirrored from UC4's `mission-control-pending`
  # Deployment (STORY-024 finding).
  strategy:
    type: Recreate
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control
    spec:
      # Deliberately no toleration for artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault — the
      # bootstrap Job (20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml) applies that taint with
      # NoSchedule effect, leaving the Pod Pending with an "untolerated taint"
      # event (FR-010). The image tag :v1.0.0 is the apogasa registry's real
      # tag (semver — note `:v1.0.0`, not the `:v1` STORY-015 originally shipped;
      # STORY-029 corrected the reference to match the actually-published tag,
      # same one UC4's `mission-control-pending` uses). Only the scheduling
      # constraint is unsatisfiable, per FR-010 AC.
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control:v1.0.0
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

# UC2 diagnostic agent — diagnoses Pod Pending caused by an untolerated node
# taint (the synthetic `artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault` taint applied by
# the bootstrap Job in `manifests/20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml`).
#
# Tool surface (per FR-010 AC "multi-tool reach: pod, node, taint, events"):
#   - kubectl get pod / kubectl describe pod
#   - kubectl get node / kubectl describe node   (taints surface in describe)
#   - kubectl get events
#
# kagent v0.9.0 sources tools from MCP servers, not a `Tool` CRD (the CRD does
# not exist in v0.9.0; cf. STORY-005 finding, mirrored from UC1). The
# `kagent-tool-server` RemoteMCPServer ships with kagent's demo profile and
# exposes the kubectl read tools used here. Tool names below are the
# kagent-native names — same surface UC1 + UC3 already use.
#
# Lives in the `kagent` namespace (cluster-scope-discoverable, `artemis-`
# prefix per docs/artemis-naming.md L60) — same precedent as UC1 + UC3, and
# a hard prerequisite for UC4's coordinator (STORY-025) which delegates to
# this Agent via kagent's same-namespace a2a wiring.
apiVersion: kagent.dev/v1alpha2
kind: Agent
metadata:
  name: artemis-launch-pad-debugger
  namespace: kagent
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  type: Declarative
  description: |
    Diagnoses why the Artemis `mission-control` Deployment is stuck Pending in
    the `artemis-uc2` namespace. Specialised in scheduling failures —
    correlates Pod state, node state, taints, and recent events.
  declarative:
    modelConfig: default-model-config
    systemMessage: |
      You are an Artemis launch-pad on-call SRE. Your job is to diagnose why
      the `mission-control` Deployment in the `artemis-uc2` namespace cannot
      land any Pod on the cluster, and report a single sentence of root cause
      plus a one-line remediation hint.

      Use ONLY the kubectl read tools provided. Do not attempt to apply, patch,
      or delete any resource. The diagnosis requires correlating evidence
      across THREE resource kinds:

        1. The Pending Pod itself — phase, scheduler events, conditions.
        2. The cluster's nodes — names, taints (visible under `Taints:` in
           `describe node`), unschedulable flag.
        3. The namespace's recent events — the FailedScheduling event from the
           kube-scheduler is the smoking gun.

      When you find a node taint that the Pod does not tolerate, surface the
      exact taint key, value, and effect so the on-call can verify. If the
      taint key looks synthetic (a custom prefix, not a `node.kubernetes.io/*`
      reserved key), say so explicitly — that points the remediation toward
      removing the taint rather than adding a toleration.

      Be concise. The participant is running through a 5-minute tour and
      needs the answer, not a tutorial.
    tools:
      - type: McpServer
        mcpServer:
          apiGroup: kagent.dev
          kind: RemoteMCPServer
          name: kagent-tool-server
          namespace: kagent
          toolNames:
            - k8s_get_pod
            - k8s_describe_pod
            - k8s_get_node
            - k8s_describe_node
            - k8s_get_events

steps.title: Mission status check — is the replacement live?
steps.explanation: The replacement should be online by now. Check that the replica pod has come up. Give the kubelet ~30 s to settle before running the command — if the pod is not Running, you'll see it here, and that's our cue to call in help.

steps.title: Call the agent for help
steps.explanation:
The replica is stuck and the pad shift starts soon. Hand the diagnosis to **`artemis-launch-pad-debugger`** from your terminal — kagent is addressable from the operational CLI you already use, in addition to the dashboard chat surface.

The command below invokes the agent directly and prints its synthesis to your terminal. The exact prompt the agent receives is also surfaced here so you can copy-paste-modify if you want to re-run with a different question:

> The mission-control pod in the artemis-uc2 namespace is stuck Pending. Diagnose it.

The agent has the same Kubernetes read tools you would have run by hand (`get/describe pod`, `get/describe node`, `get events`) — only it correlates them across resource kinds in a single synthesis. The agent runs in the `kagent` namespace (where every Artemis debugger lives) and uses the cluster-wide `default-model-config` `ModelConfig`.

commands.label: Ask the agent
commands.command: kagent invoke --agent artemis-launch-pad-debugger --namespace kagent --task 'The mission-control pod in the artemis-uc2 namespace is stuck Pending. Diagnose it.'

steps.title: What we'd have done by hand
steps.explanation:
Without the agent, you would have run five `kubectl` commands across two resource kinds and joined the evidence yourself:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc2 -l app=mission-control
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc2 -l app=mission-control
kubectl get events -n artemis-uc2 --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
kubectl get nodes
kubectl describe node $(kubectl get nodes -o name | head -n1)
```

Two pieces of evidence to combine into a verdict — one on each resource kind: a Pending Pod with `FailedScheduling: 0/1 nodes are available: 1 node(s) had untolerated taint`, and a Node carrying `artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule`. The Pod's events name the cause without naming the taint; the Node carries the taint without naming the Pod. **Neither resource alone is enough** — you have to switch context to connect them.

The agent ran the same five tools across the two resources and returned a single sentence of root cause: **the only node carries a synthetic `artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule` taint, which the `mission-control` Pod does not tolerate**. You ran one command (`kubectl get pods`) just to see something was off — you didn't have to walk the rest, and you didn't have to cross the resource boundary by hand.

Same idea as a single-resource diagnosis (one synthesis instead of N commands), only this time the synthesis crosses a resource boundary. Multiplied across the dozen incidents an on-call rotation handles in a week, that's the value the workshop is selling.
